subject: Chemistry
q:
For the following gas phase equilibrium reaction at constant temperature,
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 1 / 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
if the total pressure is $\sqrt{3} \mathrm{~atm}$ and the pressure equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}\right)$ is 9 atm, then the degree of dissociation is given as $\left(x \times 10^{-2}\right)^{-1 / 2}$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (nearest integer)
question:
For the following gas phase equilibrium reaction at constant temperature,
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 1 / 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
if the total pressure is $\sqrt{3} \mathrm{~atm}$ and the pressure equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}\right)$ is 9 atm, then the degree of dissociation is given as $\left(x \times 10^{-2}\right)^{-1 / 2}$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (nearest integer)
solution:
For the reaction: $NH_3(g) \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}N_2(g) + \frac{3}{2}H_2(g)$

At $t = 0$: 1 mole, –, –
At equilibrium: $1-\alpha$, $\frac{\alpha}{2}$, $\frac{3\alpha}{2}$
Total moles $= 1 + \alpha$

$K_P = \frac{\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)^{1/2}\left(\frac{3\alpha}{2}\right)^{3/2}}{(1-\alpha)} \cdot \left(\frac{P_T}{1+\alpha}\right)^1$

Given $P_T = \sqrt{3}$ atm and $K_P = 9$ atm:

$9 = \frac{\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)^{1/2}\left(\frac{3\alpha}{2}\right)^{3/2}}{(1-\alpha)} \times \frac{(3)^{1/2}}{1+\alpha}$

Simplifying: $9 = \frac{9\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)^2}{1-\alpha^2}$

$1 - \alpha^2 = \frac{\alpha^2}{4}$

$\frac{5\alpha^2}{4} = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha^2 = 0.8$

$\alpha = (0.8)^{1/2} = \left(\frac{1}{0.8}\right)^{-1/2} = (125 \times 10^{-2})^{-1/2}$

So $x = 125$.

subject: Chemistry
q: One mole each of He and $A(g)$ are taken in a $10$ L closed flask and heated to $400$ K to establish the following equilibrium. $A(g) \rightleftharpoons B(g)$. $K_c$ for this reaction at $400$ K is $4.0$. The partial pressures (in atm) of He and $B(g)$ are respectively (at equilibrium) (Assume He, $A(g)$ and $B(g)$ behave as ideal gases) (Given: $R = 0.082$ L atm K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1}$)
question: One mole each of He and $A(g)$ are taken in a $10$ L closed flask and heated to $400$ K to establish the following equilibrium. $A(g) \rightleftharpoons B(g)$. $K_c$ for this reaction at $400$ K is $4.0$. The partial pressures (in atm) of He and $B(g)$ are respectively (at equilibrium) (Assume He, $A(g)$ and $B(g)$ behave as ideal gases) (Given: $R = 0.082$ L atm K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1}$)
solution:
The given reaction is $A(g) \rightleftharpoons B(g)$.

Let the initial moles of $A$ be $1$ and at equilibrium, let $x$ moles of $A$ dissociate.

Moles at equilibrium:
$n_A = 1 - x$
$n_B = x$

Since $\Delta n_g = 0$, the equilibrium constant $K_c$ can be written in terms of moles:
$K_c = \dfrac{n_B}{n_A} = \dfrac{x}{1 - x} = 4.0$

$x = 4 - 4x \Rightarrow 5x = 4 \Rightarrow x = 0.8$

At equilibrium, the moles of the gases are:
$n_{He} = 1$
$n_B = 0.8$

Using the ideal gas equation $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$, the partial pressures are calculated as follows:

Partial pressure of He:
$P_{He} = \dfrac{1 \times 0.082 \times 400}{10} = 3.28$ atm

Partial pressure of $B(g)$:
$P_B = \dfrac{0.8 \times 0.082 \times 400}{10} = 2.624$ atm

Answer: $3.28, 2.624$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of correct statement/s involving equilibria in physical processes from the following is ________

(A) Equilibrium is possible only in a closed system at a given temperature.

(B) Both the opposing processes occur at the same rate.

(C) When equilibrium is attained at a given temperature, the value of all its parameters became equal

(D) For dissolution of solids in liquids, the solubility is constant at a given temperature.
question:
The number of correct statement/s involving equilibria in physical processes from the following is ________

(A) Equilibrium is possible only in a closed system at a given temperature.

(B) Both the opposing processes occur at the same rate.

(C) When equilibrium is attained at a given temperature, the value of all its parameters became equal

(D) For dissolution of solids in liquids, the solubility is constant at a given temperature.
solution:
The equilibrium is possible when there is no exchange of matter between system and surroundings.

At equilibrium state the forward and backward processes occur with same rate (speed)

When a system reaches equilibrium at a given temperature, the forward and reverse reactions are occurring at the same rate, and the concentrations (or partial pressures) of the reactants and products no longer change with time. At this point, the system has achieved a steady state, where the rate of the forward reaction is equal to the rate of the reverse reaction. So when equilibrium is attained, the value of all its parameters became constant.

The solubility is function of solubility product, the value of which is constant at a given temperature.

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the following reaction at $50°$C and at $2$ atm pressure,
$2N_2O_5(g) \rightleftharpoons 2N_2O_4(g)+O_2(g)$
$N_2O_5$ is $50\%$ dissociated.
The magnitude of standard free energy change at this temperature is $x$.
$x=$ ______ J mol$^{-1}$ [Nearest integer].
Given: $R=8.314$ J mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$, $\log 2=0.30$, $\log 3=0.48$, $\ln 10=2.303$, $°C+273=K$
question:
For the following reaction at $50°$C and at $2$ atm pressure,
$2N_2O_5(g) \rightleftharpoons 2N_2O_4(g)+O_2(g)$
$N_2O_5$ is $50\%$ dissociated.
The magnitude of standard free energy change at this temperature is $x$.
$x=$ ______ J mol$^{-1}$ [Nearest integer].
Given: $R=8.314$ J mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$, $\log 2=0.30$, $\log 3=0.48$, $\ln 10=2.303$, $°C+273=K$
solution:
Let the initial moles of $N_2O_5$ be $2$.

The given reaction is:
$2N_2O_5(g) \rightleftharpoons 2N_2O_4(g) + O_2(g)$

Since $N_2O_5$ is $50\%$ dissociated (degree of dissociation $\alpha = 0.5$), the moles at equilibrium are:
Moles of $N_2O_5 = 2(1 - 0.5) = 1$
Moles of $N_2O_4 = 2(0.5) = 1$
Moles of $O_2 = 0.5$

Total moles at equilibrium $= 1 + 1 + 0.5 = 2.5$

Given the total pressure $P = 2$ atm, the partial pressures of the gases are:
$P_{N_2O_5} = \dfrac{1}{2.5} \times 2 = 0.8$ atm
$P_{N_2O_4} = \dfrac{1}{2.5} \times 2 = 0.8$ atm
$P_{O_2} = \dfrac{0.5}{2.5} \times 2 = 0.4$ atm

The equilibrium constant $K_p$ is:
$K_p = \dfrac{(P_{N_2O_4})^2 \cdot P_{O_2}}{(P_{N_2O_5})^2} = \dfrac{(0.8)^2 \times 0.4}{(0.8)^2} = 0.4$

The standard free energy change $\Delta G^\circ$ is given by:
$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p$

Using the given values:
$\ln(0.4) = 2.303 \log(0.4) = 2.303 (\log 4 - \log 10)$
$\ln(0.4) = 2.303 (2 \log 2 - 1) = 2.303 (2 \times 0.30 - 1) = 2.303 (-0.4) = -0.9212$

Now, substituting the values into the $\Delta G^\circ$ equation ($T = 50^\circ C + 273 = 323$ K):
$\Delta G^\circ = -8.314 \times 323 \times (-0.9212)$
$\Delta G^\circ = 2473.81$ J mol$^{-1}$

The magnitude of the standard free energy change to the nearest integer is $2474$ J mol$^{-1}$.

Answer: $2474$

subject: Chemistry
q: A vessel at $1000 \mathrm{~K}$ contains $\mathrm{CO}_2$ with a pressure of $0.5 \mathrm{~atm}$. Some of the $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is converted into $\mathrm{CO}$ on the addition of graphite. If the total pressure at equilibrium is $0.8 \mathrm{~atm}$, the value of $\mathrm{K}$ is
question: A vessel at $1000 \mathrm{~K}$ contains $\mathrm{CO}_2$ with a pressure of $0.5 \mathrm{~atm}$. Some of the $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is converted into $\mathrm{CO}$ on the addition of graphite. If the total pressure at equilibrium is $0.8 \mathrm{~atm}$, the value of $\mathrm{K}$ is
solution: Total pressure at equilibrium $=0.8 \mathrm{~atm}$; Total no.of moles $=p+x$. Therefore $\mathrm{p} \propto \mathrm{n} ; \frac{0.5}{0.8}=\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=0.3$ $$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}}^2}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_2}}=\frac{0.6 \times 0.6}{0.2}=1.8 \mathrm{~atm} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: An amount of solid $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{HS}$ is placed in a flask already containing ammonia gas at a certain temperature and $0.50 \mathrm{~atm}$. Pressure. Ammonium hydrogen sulphide decomposes to yield $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ gases in the flask. When the decomposition reaction reaches equilibrium, the total pressure in the flask rises to $0.84 \mathrm{~atm}$. The equilibrium constant for $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{HS}$ decomposition at this temperature is
question: An amount of solid $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{HS}$ is placed in a flask already containing ammonia gas at a certain temperature and $0.50 \mathrm{~atm}$. Pressure. Ammonium hydrogen sulphide decomposes to yield $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ gases in the flask. When the decomposition reaction reaches equilibrium, the total pressure in the flask rises to $0.84 \mathrm{~atm}$. The equilibrium constant for $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{HS}$ decomposition at this temperature is
solution: Total pressure $=0.5+2 \mathrm{x}=0.84$ i.e., $\mathrm{x}=0.17$ $\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{NH}_3} \cdot \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~s}}$ $=(0.67) .(0.17)$ $\quad=0.1139$.

solution: As per property of equilibria reverse the equation \& divide it by 2

subject: Chemistry
q: The equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)$ for the reaction $\mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$ at temperature $\mathrm{T}$ is $4 \times 10^{-4}$. The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for the reaction, $\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow{1 / 2} \mathrm{~N}_2(\mathrm{g})+{1 / 2} \mathrm{~O}_2(\mathrm{g})$ at the same temperature is :
question: The equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)$ for the reaction $\mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$ at temperature $\mathrm{T}$ is $4 \times 10^{-4}$. The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for the reaction, $\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow{1 / 2} \mathrm{~N}_2(\mathrm{g})+{1 / 2} \mathrm{~O}_2(\mathrm{g})$ at the same temperature is :
solution: $\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{N}_2+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO} & \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=4 \times 10^{-4} \\ \mathrm{NO} \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 & \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{C}}^1=\sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{C}}}} \\ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}^1=\frac{1}{\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-4}}}=50 & \end{array}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the reaction Ag⇌Bg at 495 K, ΔrG°=-9.478 kJ mol-1

If we start the reaction in a closed container at 495 K with 22 millimoles of A, the amount of B is the equilibrium mixture is ________ millimoles. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1;ℓn10=2.303
question:
For the reaction Ag⇌Bg at 495 K, ΔrG°=-9.478 kJ mol-1

If we start the reaction in a closed container at 495 K with 22 millimoles of A, the amount of B is the equilibrium mixture is ________ millimoles. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1;ℓn10=2.303
solution:
ΔG°=-RTℓnKeq

Given ΔG°=-9.478 KJ/mole

T=495 K  R=8.314 J mol-1

So -9.478×103=-495×8.314×ℓnKeq

ℓnKeq=2.303

=ℓn10

So Keq=10

Now Ag⇌Bgt=0220t=022-xx

Keq=BC=x22-x=10

or x=20

So millimoles of B=20

(Given :<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>082</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: For the reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>(</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⇌</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>(</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>492</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>.</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></msub></math> for the reaction at same temperature is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Given :<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>082</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
The given reaction is,

 N2O4( g)⇌2NO2( g)

Kp=0.492 atm

T=300 K

KP=KC·(RT)Δng

Δng= number of gaseous molecules of products – number of gaseous molecules of reactants.

Δng=1

By putting the values in the above equation,

⇒Kc=KpRT=0.4920.082×300=2×10-2

A      +      2B      ⇌       2C    +    D
Initial conc. of [A] = a , [B] = 1.5a , [C] = 0 and [D] = 0

At equilibrium [A] = (a - x) , [B] = 1.5(a - 2x) , [C] = 2x and [D] = x

0.5 a=x

a=2x

kC=2x2xa-x1.5a-2x2=4x2.xxx2=4

n-αn    αn    αn     at equilibrium

q: One mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ and two moles of $\mathrm{SO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ were heated in a closed vessel of one-litre capacity at $1098 \mathrm{~K}$. At equilibrium $1.6$ moles of $\mathrm{SO}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}$ were found. The equilibrium constant $K_c$ of the reaction would be
question: One mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ and two moles of $\mathrm{SO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ were heated in a closed vessel of one-litre capacity at $1098 \mathrm{~K}$. At equilibrium $1.6$ moles of $\mathrm{SO}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}$ were found. The equilibrium constant $K_c$ of the reaction would be
solution:
$\quad \mathrm{O}_2+2 \mathrm{SO}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_3$ at $\mathrm{t}=0 \quad 1 \quad 2 \quad 0$ at equil. $(1-\alpha) 2(1-\alpha) \quad 2 \alpha$ Given at equilibrium
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \alpha=1.6 \\
& K_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{(2 \alpha)^2}{(1-\alpha)(2-2 \alpha)^2}=\frac{\begin{array}{c}
\alpha=0.8 \\
(0.8)^2
\end{array}}{(1-0.8)(1-0.8)^2} \\
& =\frac{0.64}{0.002} \\
& K_{\mathrm{C}}=80 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

q: For the reaction, $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{COCl}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ the $\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}}$ is equal to
question: For the reaction, $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{COCl}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ the $\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}}$ is equal to
solution: $\mathrm{Kp}=\mathrm{Kc}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The value of $K_p$ for the equilibrium reaction $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g)$ is 2 .
The percentage dissociation of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(g)$ at a pressure of $0.5 \mathrm{~atm}$ is
question:
The value of $K_p$ for the equilibrium reaction $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g)$ is 2 .
The percentage dissociation of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(g)$ at a pressure of $0.5 \mathrm{~atm}$ is
solution:
$\begin{array}{llcc}\text { } & \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \\ & \text { Initial moles } & 1 & 0 \\ & \text { Moles of equil. } & (1-\alpha) & 2 \alpha\end{array}$
( $\alpha=$ degree of dissociation $)$
Total number of moles at equil.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =(1-\alpha)+2 \alpha \\
& =(1+\alpha) \\
p_{N_2 \mathrm{O}_4} & =\frac{(1-\alpha)}{(1+\alpha)} \times P \\
p_{N O_2} & =\frac{2 \alpha}{(1+\alpha)} \times P \\
K_P & =\frac{\left(p_{N_O}\right)^2}{p_{N_2 O_4}}=\frac{\left(\frac{2 \alpha}{(1+\alpha)} \times P\right)^2}{\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right) \times P}=\frac{4 \alpha^2 P}{1-\alpha^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Given, } K_P=2, P=0.5 \mathrm{~atm} \\
& \therefore \quad K_P=\frac{4 \alpha^2 P}{1-\alpha^2} \\
& =\frac{4 \alpha^2 \times 0.5}{1-\alpha^2} \\
& \alpha=0.707 \approx 0.71 \\
& \therefore \quad \text { Percentage dissociation } \\
& =0.71 \times 100=71
\end{aligned}
$$

q:
For the following reaction, equilibrium constant are given:

Ss+O2g⇌SO2g; K1=1052

2Ss+3O2g⇌2SO3g; K2=10129

The equilibrium constant for the reaction, 2SO2g+O2g⇌2SO3g is:
question:
For the following reaction, equilibrium constant are given:

Ss+O2g⇌SO2g; K1=1052

2Ss+3O2g⇌2SO3g; K2=10129

The equilibrium constant for the reaction, 2SO2g+O2g⇌2SO3g is:
solution:
Ss+O2g⇌SO2g; k1=1052 ……(i)

2Ss+3O2g⇌SO2g; k2=10129 ……(ii)

2SO2g+O2g⇌2SO3g; keq=?

keq=SO32SO22O2

k1=SO2O2=1052

k2=SO32O23=10129

keq=k2k12=1012910522=1025

subject: Chemistry
q:
The ratio $\frac{K_p}{K_c}$ for the reaction
$\mathrm{CO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} O_2(g) \rightleftharpoons C O_2(g)$ is:
question:
The ratio $\frac{K_p}{K_c}$ for the reaction
$\mathrm{CO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} O_2(g) \rightleftharpoons C O_2(g)$ is:
solution:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}}$
For the reaction
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \leftrightharpoons \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \Delta n_g=1-\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right)=-\frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore K_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{RT}}} ; \quad \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{R T}}$

subject: Chemistry
q: For the reaction equilibrium $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ the concentrations of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ at equilibrium are $4.8 \times 10^{-2}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ respectively. The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for the reaction is
question: For the reaction equilibrium $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ the concentrations of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ at equilibrium are $4.8 \times 10^{-2}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ respectively. The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for the reaction is
solution: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right]}=\frac{\left[1.2 \times 10^{-2}\right]^2}{\left[4.8 \times 10^{-2}\right]}=3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements
Statement I : A catalyst cannot alter the equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)$ of the reaction, temperature remaining constant
Statement II : A homogenous catalyst can change the equilibrium composition of a system temperature remaining constant
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements
Statement I : A catalyst cannot alter the equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)$ of the reaction, temperature remaining constant
Statement II : A homogenous catalyst can change the equilibrium composition of a system temperature remaining constant
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II is false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution: A catalyst can change equilibrium composition if it is added at constant pressure, but it can not change equilibrium constant.

question:

(assuming ideality)
solution:
K P = K C RT Δ n g

SO 2 (g)+ 1 2 O 2 (g)⇌ SO 3 g

Δ n g =1−(1+ 1 2 )=- 1 2

subject: Chemistry
q: A vessel at 1000 K contains $\mathrm{CO}_2$ with a pressure of 0.5 atm . Some of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is converted into CO on addition of graphite. If total pressure at equilibrium is 0.8 atm , then Kp is :
question: A vessel at 1000 K contains $\mathrm{CO}_2$ with a pressure of 0.5 atm . Some of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is converted into CO on addition of graphite. If total pressure at equilibrium is 0.8 atm , then Kp is :
solution:
$\begin{array}{lc}\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons & 2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}) \\ 0.5 & - \\ 0.5-\mathrm{x} & 2 \mathrm{x}\end{array}$


$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{P}_{\text {total }}=0.5+\mathrm{x}=0.8 \\  \mathrm{x}=0.3 \\  \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{(0.6)^2}{0.2}=1.8\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: For standardizing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> solution, which of the following is used as a primary standard?
question: For standardizing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> solution, which of the following is used as a primary standard?
solution:
A primary standard in metrology is a standard that is sufficiently accurate such that it is not calibrated by or subordinate to other standards. Primary standards are defined via other quantities like length, mass and time. Primary standards are used to calibrate other standards referred to as working standards.

Oxalic acid is used as a primary standard for NaOH standardization.

solution:
It is heterogeneous equilibrium Fe is liquid Fe2O3  is solid whose active mass is taken to be unity so, it will not be considered in kp, kc,& kx expresssion.
So, it will not disturb the equilibrium

subject: Chemistry
q: The equilibrium constant at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> for a reaction <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>⇌</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math>. If the initial concentration of all the four species were<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> each, then the equilibrium concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> (in mol <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) will be:
question: The equilibrium constant at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> for a reaction <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>⇌</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math>. If the initial concentration of all the four species were<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> each, then the equilibrium concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> (in mol <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) will be:
solution:
K at 298 K for the reaction
A+B ⇌C+D is 100.

Given: The initial concentration of all four species is 1 M.
A+B⇌C+Dt=01111at equilbrium1-x1-x1+x1+x

K=1+x 1+x1-x 1-x=100
⇒ 1+x1-x=10
⇒ x=911=0.818
⇒ D=1+x=1+0.818=1.818

subject: Chemistry
q:
The effect of addition of helium gas to the following reaction in equilibrium state at constant volume, is :
PCI5( g)⇌PCl3( g)+Cl2( g)
question:
The effect of addition of helium gas to the following reaction in equilibrium state at constant volume, is :
PCI5( g)⇌PCl3( g)+Cl2( g)
options: the equilibrium will shift in the forward direction and more of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>PCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> gases will be produced.
the equilibrium will go backward due to suppression of dissociation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>PCl</mi><mn>5</mn></msub></math>.
addition of helium will not affect the equilibrium.
solution:
PCI5( g)⇌PCl3( g)+Cl2( g)
In the above reaction vessel, when helium(an inert gas) is added following cases can happen for isobaric and isochoric processes respectively:

Case 1 : At constant P - volume will increase so reaction will shift in forward direction then answer will be A

Case 2 : At constant volume no change in active mass so reaction will not shift in any direction then answer will be D.

Thus, at constant volume, addition of helium will not have any impact on the equilibrium.
